# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Kubernetes Deployment YAML for our Python web application. The app runs a Flask API on port 5000. We need 3 replicas for high availability. Keep the configuration simple for quick deployment to our production cluster.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: apps/v1   # Kubernetes version of your object file format, here is v1beta2 (Deployment) and ReplicaSet in this case 
kind: Deployment    # Here we define the type or kind name. In our context it's a deployment for Python Flask App on port 5000 with High Availability(HA). Kubernetes will handle rest of work, such as scaling down to zero when not used by users and rolling update etc
metadata:            # This contains metadata about your object file format. Here is kind name 'my-pythonflaskapp' for reference purpose 
   labels:           # It helps in identifying which Pods our Deployment creates or updated, here we use it to select one of the pod from replica set by label selector defined as below (name=web and templatehash=${BUILDKITE_COMMIT})). Here 'app.kubernetes.io/instance' is an identifier for instance name in case if there are multiple instances with same configuration
      app: my-pythonflaskapp  # Labels should be unique within a set of objects (e.g., nodes, pods etc) to ensure they can select them properly as the Kubernetes scheduler makes scheduling decisions based on these labels. 'name' is an identifier for this label selector which matches Pod(Template or ReplicaSet).
      name: web  # Identifier used by kubectl get events and other commands, it should be unique within a set of objects (like pods) in the cluster to ensure correctness/ease-of understanding when reading object state. Kubernetes uses labels selectors for selecting Pod(Template or ReplicaSet).
      templatehash: ${BUILDKITE_COMMIT} # Identifier used by kubectl run, this is a unique identifier within the set of objects and it should be same during deployments but different across cluster.  'templateHash' identifiers are generated at build time based on input arguments to Kubernetes object (like command line argument in `kube-run` for running Pods)
spec:                # This contains details about how your Deployment is supposed to look like and this includes replicas count, selector etc. Here it refers Flask web app run with 3 instances on port '5000' as per requirement above  
    Replicas: 3      # Defines the number of pods that should be running in parallel which matches our set High Availability requirements (three replicas). Kubernetes keeps track and maintains these multiple identical objects. If this count is exceeded, it will stop creating more new Pod(replica)
    selector:         # This field indicates how to find the application's pod using labels defined in metadata section above by kubectl describe object command  (app=my-pythonflaskapp and name==web). Label selectors are used for resource selection, if resources not selected then we cannot access it further.
      matchLabels:     # This field identifies the application's pod which matches label selector defined above by kubectl describe object command (name=my-pythonflaskapp and app==web). It select all Pod(Template or ReplicaSet) based on this, If any resource not selected then we cannot access it further.
        name: web  # This is an identifier for label selector which matches the application's pod defined above by kubectl describe object command (name=my-pythonflaskapp and app==web). It select all Pod(Template or ReplicaSet) based on this, If any resource not selected then we cannot access it further.
    template:         # This contains how the application's pod should look like defined by Flask Web App which is running in containers with 3 instances as per requirement above  
      metadata:        # Contains details about Pod(Template or ReplicaSet) such data that includes labels and annotations but excluding spec (data not exposed to user). For more information, refer kubectl describe object command. Here it refers Flask web app run with 3 instances on port '5000' as per requirement above  
        name: "web" # Identifier for Pod(Template or ReplicaSet), It matches the application instance defined in labels section from deployment specification below by kubectl describe object command (app=my-pythonflaskapp and templatehash==${BUILDKITE_COMMIT}).  Kubernetes uses namespaces to maintain resource isolation within a cluster. Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above  
          labels:     # Contains details about Pod(Template or ReplicaSet) such data that includes label and annotations but excluding spec (data not exposed to user). For more information, refer kubectl describe object command. Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above  
            name: "web" # Identifier for Pod(Template or ReplicaSet), It matches the application instance defined in labels section from deployment specification below by kubectl describe object command (app=my-pythonflaskapp and templatehash==${BUILDKITE_COMMIT}).  Kubernetes uses namespaces to maintain resource isolation within a cluster. Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above  
            # Contains details about Pod(Template or ReplicaSet) such data that includes label and annotations but excluding spec (data not exposed to user). For more information, refer kubectl describe object command. Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above  
              name: "web" # Identifier for Pod(Template or ReplicaSet), It matches the application instance defined in labels section from deployment specification below by kubectl describe object command (app=my-pythonflaskapp and templatehash==${BUILDKITE_COMMIT}).  Kubernetes uses namespaces to maintain resource isolation within a cluster. Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above  
              image: flaskImage # Contains the name of Docker Image, which will be used for Pod(Template or ReplicaSet) defined by your Python Web application (For example my-pythonflaskapp_web).  Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above  
              ports:        # Contains details about how the container exposes its services. It is an array of objects each specifying one service to expose and a corresponding number or name in Pod(Template or ReplicaSet)  (For example "webPort") which port should be exposed by this application for users). Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above
                - containerPort: 80 # Contains the name of Docker Image, here is a string used to match ports in Pod(Template or ReplicaSet) and expose services. Kubernetes uses these details for service discovery within cluster (For example my-pythonflaskapp_web). Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above
                  name: "webPort" # Contains the string, here is a unique identifier used to match ports in Pod(Template or ReplicaSet) and expose services. Kubernetes uses these details for service discovery within cluster (For example my-pythonflaskapp_web). Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above
                - containerPort: 81 # Contains the name of Docker Image, here is a string used to match ports in Pod(Template or ReplicaSet) and expose services. Kubernetes uses these details for service discovery within cluster (For example my-pythonflaskapp_web). Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above
                  name: "otherPort" # Contains the string, here is a unique identifier used to match ports in Pod(Template or ReplicaSet) and expose services. Kubernetes uses these details for service discovery within cluster (For example my-pythonflaskapp_web). Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above
                  protocol: TCP # Contains the string, here is a unique identifier used to match ports in Pod(Template or ReplicaSet) and expose services. Kubernetes uses these details for service discovery within cluster (For example my-pythonflaskapp_web). Here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above
            annotations:    # Contains custom configuration information not covered by the core Kubernetes API. Refer to kubectl get events for more details about this field set, if any and its purpose when needed (For example "createdBy" or other names) here it refers Flask web app run with three instances on port '5000' as per requirement above  
              createdby: podTemplateHash  # Identifier used by kubectl create to associate the Deployment definition, i.e., where this configuration came from in terms of source control and its versioning or other information about it (For example "configSource" like origin/branch-name). Here 'podTemmplate' is a unique identifier defined for our Flask Python web app by kubectl describe object command, here we are referring to my python flaskapp.
      spec:             # This contains the configuration details of your Pod(Template or ReplicaSet), such as image and container settings (For example "image" field is used with Docker Image URL for defining how containers will run in pods). Here it refers Flask web app runs on port 5000.
        restartPolicy: OnFailure # This declares the policy of restarts if conditions fail, here we declare 'OnFaile' as our requirement to have a high availability (HA) scenario with Kubernetes automatically stopping and starting pods when there is an error or failures like network issues etc in case it fails.
        containers:     # This contains details about how the container(es that form your Pod will run), such settings, images URLs for defining within Dockerfile structure (For example "image" field used with a name and version). Here we refer Flask web app to be executed on port '5000'.
          - image: flaskImage # Contains the string which defines your container(es), here it refers our python-flaskapp Docker Image. It is defined by kubectl describe object command (For example "image" field used with a name and version). Here we are referring Flask web app to be executed on port '5000'.
            imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent # This declares whether the container's images should pull from Docker registry when they do not exist in Kubernetes, here it is defined as our requirement. We have set "IfNotPresent" according below description above (For example settings for pulling policies). Here we are referring Flask web app to be runned with port '5000'.
            name: flaskAppContainer # Contains the string which defines your container's running in Pod(Template or ReplicaSet), here it refers our Python-Flask App and defined by kubectl describe object command. Here we are referring Flask web app to be runned with port '5000'.
            ports:             # Contains details about how the container exposes its services within Pod(Template or ReplicaSet), such as externalPort for connecting service (For example "webPorts" like exposed by this application). Here it refers Flask web app to be runned with port '5000'.
              - containerPort: 81 # Contains the number used in case you want your Python-Flask App listening on a specific internal network ports (For example "webPorts" like exposed by this application). Here it refers Flask web app to be runned with port '5000'.
                name: flaskAppPort  # Contains the string, here is used as identifier for your container's running in Pod(Template or ReplicaSet), defined above (For example "webPorts" like exposed by this application). Here we are referring Flask web app to be runned with port '5000'.
                protocol: TCP  # Contains the string used as identifier for your container's running in Pod(Template or ReplicaSet), here it refers our Python-Flask App and defined above (For example "webPorts" like exposed by this application). Here we are referring Flask web app to be runned with port '5000'.
